The money connection. When I got in the seminar business, it's a good story. Everybody else was selling seminars for $500. My first ones were $5,000 and I moved to $15,000 and $20,000. And we actually, I mean the truth is it's not a lie. I stopped them because I'm like one of these people that gets tired and I burned out after doing a thousand people at an average of $18,000. I'm not saying it to brag, I'm saying it to demonstrate the power of what I'm going to say. Everyone else would run ads in the newspaper or they would run stupid little one minute spot on TV. I would figure you already had the entrepreneur market and I was lucky because I'd worked with them and I was able to get every financial newsletter I'd ever helped. I got Tony Robbins who I helped. I got Success Magazine who I helped. I got all people to write letters extraordinarily lawdatory about me but what I did first was more strategic. I would first go to all these lists and have them send a letter telling them how great I was and I get $5,000 an hour when I consult. I don't do much of it but it's $40,000 a day if they come to me and if I go to them it's $50,000 or $60,000. And we'd send a letter out first selling $50,000 and I'd just be tickled if I got one. If it cost me $40,000 I got back $40,000 I was happier than could be because then we'd go back with a letter that would talk about how many people would write and say how many people were so intrigued they wish they could afford it. It's way out of their realm but they begged, borrowed and steal and wanted to know is there any way the endorser could work out some more accessible way and he was delighted that Jade decided because of the economies of scale to do one for three days for only $5,000 because I didn't really need the money. They wouldn't even have their check deposited till the halfway point on the second day and if they wanted to they could even pay a modest amount that wouldn't be deposited the rest after they went home and profited and before they would be obligated when they signed up we would send them grounding materials worth more than they were even going to expend and then we did a couple other things the coolest thing I was taking a one on the coolest thing I ever did and it wasn't profitable it was strategic. Breathe did you guys just breathe just stop and get that and don't don't lose track did you catch the red herring I mean if somebody bought the $40,000 thing it was okay but it doesn't make any difference it was it was exactly it was setting it up it was laying the groundwork so that what would otherwise have been an instantly rejected proposition now was a proposition and a price that appeared eminently reasonable in a bargain and therefore made that campaign work there's a direct lesson there for you on our case presentation conversation you can just steal it yeah and there's three other connections and I can go on and I want to start back up thank you we had everything I did was strategic so we also would negotiate monster great deals with the in-flight magazines Delta air when it was big and united and success and we would run 20 page it would look like an insert but just a basically a shaded on page but it would tell an incredible story about me it would have validity but the thing that made it so powerful was it would have three full pages of testimonials with pictures but that isn't what made it and they would have daytime phone numbers you could call daytime phone numbers you know everyone goes yeah rs from Ohio and we never made much money on it but it was the reinforcing because most of the entrepreneurs it was our strategy that we were reaching with all the direct mail travel and it was very effective um I mean I could go on and on you're not good because I'm watching the clock okay so and they already got enough and the hell with the prepared notes but uh but I want to hear the halberd and revelle story okay I'll tell you because it's it's a I use it it's going to take a few minutes but even though we don't get everything it's so illustrative um two guys that we both know one's deceased and one is one is much more strategic than the other one is a much better copywriter than the other the better copywriter is the tactically minded guy the other one strategic so the tactically minded superior copywriter looks at the business world besides he wants to sell cubics or conan so he sits down he creates a company called the Beverly Hills Diamond Company writes a really compelling a letter selling a one-carat loose $39 cubics or conan that he calls the Beverly Hills Diamond uh and my numbers are going to be off but the essence are right so he spends 30 grand on an LA Times full-page ad it brings 42 after refunds and costs he makes six or seven and he takes his bow and leaves
